messages.author: Brent
messages.text: A deterministic MeshCore network and firmware simulator. The simulator uses firmware sources compiled on windows/linux to simulate chosen topologies and traffic patterns with the capability of collecting metrics about the behavior of the network. Nodes can be accessed over TCP using the MeshCore app or their CLI. https://github.com/Brent-A/mcsim
messages.content_html: A deterministic MeshCore network and firmware simulator. The simulator uses firmware sources compiled on windows/linux to simulate chosen topologies and traffic patterns with the capability of collecting metrics about the behavior of the network. Nodes can be accessed over TCP using the MeshCore app or their CLI. https://github.com/Brent-A/mcsim

messages.author: Brent
messages.text: Hello! This project is working well enough that I can start collecting some feedback. You may expect to find issues when simulating larger meshes still without some tweaking. In realtime mode you can setup mesh and connect one or more phones to companions in the mesh using the Wifi connection option in the app. In this mode you can generate and observe your own traffic. You can also attach bots like https://github.com/agessaman/meshcore-bot and interact with them. In duration mode you can simulate an arbitrary duration of time and the simulation will advance as fast as your computer allows. For quiet meshes you can simulate quickly multi-day periods, or for really busy meshes it may run slower than realtime on your computer. Unless you are taking external input from an app/bot the simulation is completely determinisitc. You can re-run the simulation with the same seed to get the same results, which is helpful in cases where you are debugging or trying to select different metrics. The simulation has a built-in metrics system that collects a variety of data about the mesh. Packet transmissions, receptions, collisions, airtime for example. Each metric type can be broken out by node, packet type, and other metrics. For example you can examine the percentage of nodes that receive a particular flood or get flood latency statistics. The set of metrics is a first draft of what may be useful and may need to evolve for different scenarios. The network is configured through layered yaml files, so you can add behaviors or modify base topologies for different test applications. There are dozens of properties that affect the setup and behavior of nodes. Companion nodes can be configured with autonomous "agents" that generate traffic in configurable patterns. There are also some tools to assist in building topology models using captured ADVERT data with a built-in terrain based radio propagation estimation model.

messages.author: Brent
messages.text: If you have more details feel free to post an issue and I can put it on my list of things to add. I'm not familiar with the MQTT usage needs. If you need to simulate a specific MQTT firmware that's possible, or it's also possible that the simulation itself could capture/inject packets from MQTT if that's what's needed.

messages.author: Brent
messages.text: Yes. It's a bit of a work in progress though. Right now I have a tool that uses a collection of node advert data from letsmesh.net to model a region. I'm using a terrain model to estimate the viable paths and signal strengths for the mesh. There's an example of the seattle region in the repositry. It has the actual node names and locations and will generate keys for each node that match their real prefixes, so the simulated environment looks reasonably similar to reality. I would like to be able to use more data from the analyzer to determine actual paths and SNR values based on recordings of traces and path records. I'm not sure if there is enough data is already collected though; it might mean probing the mesh with traces to build out an accurate model.
messages.content_html: Yes. It's a bit of a work in progress though. Right now I have a tool that uses a collection of node advert data from letsmesh.net to model a region. I'm using a terrain model to estimate the viable paths and signal strengths for the mesh. There's an example of the seattle region in the repositry. It has the actual node names and locations and will generate keys for each node that match their real prefixes, so the simulated environment looks reasonably similar to reality. I would like to be able to use more data from the analyzer to determine actual paths and SNR values based on recordings of traces and path records. I'm not sure if there is enough data is already collected though; it might mean probing the mesh with traces to build out an accurate model.

messages.author: mattzz
messages.text: In order to simulate regio/scope behavior I was playing around with the simple topology. Using meshcli I was able to connect to companions, send messages between them. However, trying to login to a Repeater via login Repeater1 '' and to Repeater1 I was unable to get results for basic commands like ver or clock like I would with a real companion/repeater pair. Is that a limitation in functionality of the simulation or am I doing it the wrong way connecting to a Repeater in order to change Repeater configuration? (edited)
messages.content_html: In order to simulate regio/scope behavior I was playing around with the simple topology. Using meshcli I was able to connect to companions, send messages between them. However, trying to login to a Repeater via login Repeater1 '' and to Repeater1 I was unable to get results for basic commands like veror clocklike I would with a real companion/repeater pair. Is that a limitation in functionality of the simulation or am I doing it the wrong way connecting to a Repeater in order to change Repeater configuration? (edited)

messages.author: Brent
messages.text: You should be able to connect to a repeater directly through its CLI via the exposed port. The simulation sets the administrator password for the repeaters to admin by default, and you should be able to manage them from a companion. You can also add a cli/commands simulation property to the repeaters that is a list of CLI commands that will be sent to the repeater on startup.
messages.content_html: You should be able to connect to a repeater directly through its CLI via the exposed port. The simulation sets the administrator password for the repeaters to admin by default, and you should be able to manage them from a companion. You can also add a cli/commands simulation property to the repeaters that is a list of CLI commands that will be sent to the repeater on startup.

messages.text: . Also note that by default mcsim prepopulates the contact lists on clients so that adverts aren't necessary to send messages initially. You can change this by setting the contacts property to an empty list.

Brent
. Also note that by default mcsim prepopulates the contact lists on clients so that adverts aren't necessary to send messages initially. You can change this by setting the contacts property to an empty list.

messages.author: Brent
messages.text: Thank you! It's still very rough, but if you want to try adapting it to your local mesh you may want to try the mcsim build-model command. It takes a json with a list of nodes where you supply name, public_key, mode (Repeater/Companion/Room_Server), and location. From that it will generate a model yaml with all the links using a terrain based model.
messages.content_html: Thank you! It's still very rough, but if you want to try adapting it to your local mesh you may want to try the mcsim build-model command. It takes a json with a list of nodes where you supply name, public_key, mode (Repeater/Companion/Room_Server), and location. From that it will generate a model yaml with all the links using a terrain based model.
